title: Boudin blanc
subtitle: gently pan-fried white sausage with mashed potato
excerpt: Boudin blanc is a pale, delicately seasoned French sausage made from pork, cream, and sometimes poultry or veal. It needs nothing more than a slow turn in butter to warm through without splitting. Serve it with a soft mash or braised greens for a quiet winter supper.
sourceNotes:
A. Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). Boudin blanc preparation and cooking method, noting the delicacy required when pan-frying to avoid splitting the casing. Public domain.

Auguste Colombié, Nouvelle Cuisine Bourgeoise (1906). Boudin blanc with potato accompaniment and seasoning guidance. Public domain.

Note: boudin blanc is not in the master ingredient lookup. The slug sausages-pork (pork sausages) is used as the closest available match. A dedicated boudin-blanc ingredient entry would improve accuracy.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The mashed potato can be made an hour ahead and kept warm over a pan of hot water, covered with a lid.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French white sausage made from finely ground pork, cream, and sometimes poultry or veal, seasoned with white pepper and nutmeg. It is much more delicate in texture and flavour than a regular pork sausage and is traditionally eaten at Christmas and throughout the winter months.

content.text: Boudin blanc is a gentle thing: a pale, cream-coloured sausage seasoned with white pepper and a little nutmeg, cooked slowly in butter so the casing does not split.

is made from very finely ground pork and sometimes poultry or veal, blended with cream and eggs to give a smooth, almost custard-like texture inside. It is very different from the robust British pork banger and needs to be handled accordingly.

The method is almost meditative: low heat, plenty of butter, and patience. The sausages are pricked once with a pin to allow steam to escape, then turned slowly in the pan over 20 minutes. The casing goes from pale to lightly golden without ever colouring aggressively. Serve with a soft mash made with warm milk and plenty of butter, and the kitchen will smell like a Paris bistro in December.

items.prepNote: boudin blanc preferred; use the best quality white sausage available
floury variety, peeled and cut into chunks

content.text: A regular 26 cm frying pan works fine. You do not need cast iron; in fact a lighter pan is easier to manage at low heat. Have a fine-tooth skewer or cocktail pin to hand for pricking the casings.

Boil the {{potato}} potato chunks in well-salted water for 18 to 20 minutes until completely tender when pierced. Drain and let them steam-dry in the colander for 2 minutes. Pass through a ricer or mash thoroughly, then beat in the {{whole-milk}} of warm milk and {{unsalted-butter}} of butter for the mash. Season with salt, white pepper, and a little freshly grated nutmeg. Keep warm covered over a low heat.

Prick each sausage once or twice with a fine pin. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter for the sausages in the frying pan over low to medium-low heat. When the butter foams, lay in the {{sausages-pork}} sausages. Cook for 18 to 20 minutes in total, turning every 4 to 5 minutes to colour evenly on all sides. The heat should stay low enough that the butter barely sizzles; if it browns quickly, lower the heat. The sausages are done when they feel firm and have turned an even light gold all over.
Spoon a generous pile of mash onto each plate and lay two sausages alongside. Pour the buttery pan juices over the top. Any braised endive or buttered leeks make a good companion here.

items.cause: Heat too high, or sausages not pricked
items.fix: Reduce the heat immediately and prick the casings with a fine pin before the next batch; boudin blanc must be cooked slowly or the steam inside bursts the skin

items.cause: Not turned frequently enough, or pan spots hotter in the centre
items.fix: Turn every 4 to 5 minutes and rotate the sausages around the pan so each piece spends time over the hottest part

items.cause: Potatoes overworked or milk added cold
items.fix: Use warm milk, not cold; always warm the milk first. Mash the potato while it is still very hot and work it as little as possible after the milk goes in

items.symptom: Sausage is pink and cold at the centre
items.cause: Cooking time too short, or heat too low to penetrate
items.fix: Keep cooking over slightly higher heat, turning often. Check the centre reaches 75°C with a probe thermometer

content.text: Boudin blanc is the French Christmas sausage. Markets across northern France and Belgium sell it from late November through to January, and it appears on festive menus alongside foie gras and oysters as part of the réveillon tradition. The version from Rethel in the Ardennes has protected status and contains only pork, eggs, cream, and seasoning, with no cereal filler. Richer and more varied versions add chicken or truffle, but the plain pork boudin eaten simply with mash on a cold evening is the one most worth knowing.
